comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will more than likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The photo? A hundred years ago you wouldn't have to ask, because she was the world's bestselling author. Her name was Marie Corelli. (Well, actually, her name was Mary Mackay, and she was the illegitimate daughter of the same Charles Mackay who wrote Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds, but Marie Corelli was the name she went by.) She was a Rosicrucian -- a member of the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis, to be precise -- and a student of occultism, and her novels, whch were wildly popular, were chockfull of bits of occult lore. That's one of the main reasons why she's been erased from literary history. Can't have occultists haunting the hallowed halls of literature!

If your local library system hasn't purged its collection, they might have a few of her books. I'm reading one novel of hers right now -- The Secret Power -- and so far, despite a certain lushness of prose and a habit of talking to the reader, it's a pleasant read with fewer adjectives than her younger contemporary H.P. Lovecraft.

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

With that said, have at it!

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- see you next week!***

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As a former personal trainer, I have some thoughts on 2:

Many martial arts have an explicit component of energy work that will carry over directly into magical practice. This does raise the issue of if the two systems are compatible. I would be very hesitatnt to mix Tai Chi with DMH practices, and I understand this has caused Greer some trouble in the past. I know an embarrassing number of martial artists who actually have some capacity to move energy around and do something with it but are also completely useless in a fight. Purely physical arts like MMA or Wrestling don't have any direct practices like this but can be useful for reasons below, essentially by absorbing elements of dance.

Dance, in my limited experience, is almost completely devoid of any direct energy work. Despite this, the focus on quality of motion has immediate and obvious applications in ritual. Getting your arm over your head is easy, getting your arm over your head in a smooth and graceful manner is not. In practice, it is really hard to look good without some control over your etheric body. This is further cultivated with breath control which I do know is a major component of Russian Ballet.

Weightlifting isn't as straightforward an issue as the other two disciplines. As stated, bad martial artists can sometimes do good energy work and nobody lasts long in dance if they can't pick up a sense of correct motion and quality of motion; weightlifting has a much lower skill floor in this respect as it is possible to mindlessly heave around large chunks of metal. A bad weightlifter may last for several years of training, but small problems turn into injuries and injuries turn into chronic health problems. If you lift like an idiot, you will hurt yourself like an idiot. In contrast, good weightlifting demands highly cultivated focus (control of arousal is a major topic in sports psychology) and coordinated movement across the entire body to both maximize force exerted, minimize tension on sensitive parts of the body, and ensure that both the weight and exercised muscles are properly supported. All this is supplemented with some number of careful active recovery techniques and possibly a highly structured diet. This might not be what the average occultist works on, but this discipline is extremely applicable, and the high level of sensitivity to the body can easily be extended to new topics.

Also, regarding foam rollers; they do work, but a trivial investment in studying massage therapy will make you capable of doing far more. It's a very stereotypical case of coming up with a gadget that can do something that humans are naturally capable of doing a much better job of.

If you want any more detail, just ask. Good luck!

I wish to give you an update on the problem I brought to you last Magic Monday, about my having common episodes of disassociation. As you suggested, I began to increase my red meat intake (not over-much, though!), and have not had any episodes since. So, thank you for that.

replies.title: Re: An Earthly Update
replies.content:
1) Huysmans is interesting but he's a devout Catholic intellectual and so his attitude toward magic is one-sided and biased. His insistence that the only magic that really counts is Satanism is straight out of the Catholic playbook -- and of course that claim is complete garbage, but it's popular garbage within that ideological stance.

2) Wagner was a horrible human being. So? He was a brilliant musician, and his music marks the culmination and the end of the classical Western musical tradition. He took it as far as it could go, and sometimes just a bit further. The Der Ring des Nibelungen tetralogy and Parsifal are among the supreme creative works of Western culture. Read a few books on Wagner, listen (if you haven't done so already) to other Romantic composers and other 19th century operas, and then definitely give him a listen.

comments.content:
Marie Corelli!!! What a treat to see her remembered here! Thank you! I'm glad to see that you like what you're reading of hers.

Her novels were arguably the first "best-sellers" ever, a phenomenon which changed the shape of the publishing industry. I had not known she was a member of the FRC; thank you for that new piece of information.

She was one of my mother's mother's favorite novelists and also one of her major sources for esoteric teaching. (Another was H. Rider Haggard, whose personal religious views were highly unconventional and esoteric.) She even named her two children after characters in Corelli's novels; my mother was Edris (from Ardath and my aunt was Muriel (from Ziska).

Academics who study the history of esotericism usually overlook the really popular novelists, to their detriment. (And genealogists generally pay little attention to the interests and personalities of the ancestors whom they study, equally to their detriment.)

There are a few very recent exceptions to the generalization I just made. Brenda Ayres and Sarah E. Maier recently edited Reinventing Marie Corelli for the Twenty-First Century (2019), a volume of articles with a few on her esoteric interests. Also a man whose legal name actually seems to be Simon Magus recently won his PhD with a dissertation titled H. Rider Haggard and the imperial Occult (2018), and just now (2021) published as a book. These two volumes are promising developments, at long last.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for this! I've started a course of reading old occult fiction from the 19th and early 20th centuries, with an eye toward future novel projects. Corelli's books have been on the shelf in every single Theosophical library I've ever visited, so she was high on the list. Regrettably, the Corelli anthology is from an academic press and costs $125, so I'll have to pass on it, but Mr. Magus' dissertation was easy to find and download -- thank you for that also.

If you happen to know of other occult-themed novelists I might not have heard of, I'm all ears... ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sax Rohmer comes immediately to mind. Don't bother with his 13 pot-boiler Fu-Manchu novels; he wrote them for the money they brought in. The same is true of his 5 Sumuru novels--Sumuru is basically a female version of Fu Manchu.

Instead, I'd recommend The Brood of the Witch Queen (1918), The Dream Deterctive (1920), Grey Face (1924) and The Bat Flies Low (1935) for starters, all of which are quite good reads.. (The last of the four can be found online at www.fadedpage.com, a Canadian site for works that have entered the public domain in that country.) More expressly esoteric, but a little more dull, are The Orchard of Tears (1918) and Wulfheim (1950).

Rohmer also wrote a number of non-fiction articles on such topics as astral projection, magic and music, and the "occult East," as well as an entire popularizing book on the history of magic (The Romance of Sorcery, 1914.) His astral projection article strikes me as particularly good.

Despite what many websites say, Rohmer does not seem ever to have been a member of The Golden Dawn. He may have belonged to the S.R.I.A., since one of his occult mentors, the alchemist and physician R. Watson Councell, was a member.

Another author is Talbot Mundy, who lived in the Theosophical community at Point Loma (near San Diego, Calif.). I heard about him from my own first esoteric teacher outside of my own family, C. H. Harvey, whose childhood was spent within that same community, and who was something of a Mundy fan. Yet another is Abraham Merritt. Both of these authors, like Rohmer, were born in the late 1870s or early 1880s, and thus were about a quarter of a century younger than Corelli and Haggard.

replies.content:
This omnibus may be of possible interest to you and others, it came out last year.

Fighters of fear :occult detective stories, edited by Mike Ashley.

Contents:
Green Tea / Joseph Sheridan Le Fanu -- The Shining pyramid / Arthur Machen -- The Haunted child / Arabella Kenealy -- The Mystery of the Felwyn Tunnel / L. T. Meade & Robert Eustace -- The Story of Yand Manor House / E & H Heron -- The Tapping on the wainscot / Allan Upward -- Samaris / Robert W. Chambers -- The Whistling room / William Hope Hodgson -- The Woman with the crooked nose / Victor Rousseau -- The Sorcerer of Arjuzanx / Max Rittenberg -- The Ivory statue / Sax Rohmer -- The Stranger / Claude & Alice Askew -- The Swaying vision / Jessie Douglas Kerruish -- The Sanatorium / F. Tennyson Jesse -- The Villa on the Borderive Road / Rose Champion de Crespigny -- The Room of fear / Ella Serymsour -- The Seven fires / Philippa Forest -- The Subletting of the mansion / Dion Fortune -- The Jest of Warburg Tantavul / Seabury Quinn -- The Soldier / A. M. Burrage -- The Horror of the height / Sydney Horler -- The Mystery of iniquity / L. Adams Beck -- The Thought monster / Amelia Reynolds Long -- The Shut room / Henry S. Whitehead -- Dr. Muncing , exorcist / Gordon MacCreagh -- The Case of the haunted cathedral / Margery Lawrence -- The Shonokins / Manly Wade Wellman -- The Dead of winter apparition / Joseph Payne Brennan -- The Garden of Paris / Eric Williams -- St. Michael & all angels / Mark Valentine -- Jeremiah / Jessica Amanda Salmonson.

Summary:
A Retrospective Collection of Classic Occult and Supernatural Detective Stories by Some of the Field's Greatest and Best-Known Weird Fiction Authors. Since the gaslit nights at the end of the nineteenth century, the occult detective has been a beloved and recurring archetype. Mixing the best aspects of the detective tale and weird or supernatural fiction, and capitalizing in part on the massive popularity of Sherlock Holmes, these stories portrayed men and women pitted against surreal and horrifying foes, usually with little to defend them but their own savvy, experience, and know-how. From William Hope Hodgson's Thomas Carnacki, to Seabury Quinn's fearless Frenchman Jules de Grandin, to Jessica Salmonson's Penelope Pettiweather, the occult detective has taken a variety of forms, investigated a wide array of supernatural and otherworldly cases, and entertained generations of readers. This new collection compiles thirty-one all-time classic occult detective stories as it traces the genre's growth from its nineteenth-century origins to the late twentieth century, showcasing the work of acclaimed pioneers of weird tales alongside cult favorites and exciting modern talents.
-

It's a very nice overview... and with so many stories in the public domain, it would be cool to see more stuff like this getting reprinted.

Marie Corelli! It so happens that, by way of reading a biography of the American artist Edward Gorey (Born to Be Posthumous), I recently delved into his favorite reading, the Mapp and Lucia novels, by E.F. Benson, and there found the character, apparently inspired by none other than Marie Corelli, the infamous Italian, baby-talking, Moonlight sonata playing Lucia (Mrs Lucas) of the most quaintly Elizabethan village of Riseholme.

The Mapp & Lucia novels, from the 1920s and 1930s, were new to me, but it turns out that I'm late to the parade, for many of my friends are long-time fans, and two series have made for British television. I would describe the Mapp and Lucias as light, rainy-day reading, albeit masterpieces of the genre. The writing is crisp and wild, and I laughed out loud at some point on almost every page. Edward Gorey read and reread the Mapp & Lucias obsessively-- and the people in his artwork, most especially those wrapped up like tamales their huge fur coats, certainly could serve as illustrations for these novels.

Now how does this connect to Magic Monday? I don't know that it does! But perhaps another synchronicity might be relevant to mention: that E.F. Benson wrote his Mapp and Lucia novels in Lamb House, which had previously been the residence of Henry James', the famous novelist (The Ambassadors, Portrait of a Lady & etc) and brother of William James, a great pioneer of consciousness studies.

As Lucia and Marie might say, "Grazie per aver ospitato i Magic Mondays"

MILLICENTLY LURKING

comments.title: Money, a cat's passing, Druidry permission, and a God-filled world
comments.content:
Good afternoon, JMG! I've had a number of questions percolating within me, and they've finally reached critical mass. Please consider these questions to be open to the community as well, as I'm interested in everyone's opinions.

1. On money. Preface: my stay-at-home wife and I have just had a third child. We're all doing well. Unfortunately, here in the US, paternity leave is pretty much nonexistent, so I only had 3 days off to help out and bond with the new kid (whose name is Auden - Welsh for "old friend", iirc), and then it's back to the proverbial mines for me. I say proverbial because I'm squarely a member of the PMC and make a middle-of-the-road middle-class income.

That's the setup. Here's the question: I have a close friend who works for one of the big tech giants. He makes probably 5 times the amount I do. We were chatting and he was shocked and appalled by the lack of rest my entire family has been allotted to introduce a new member to our family. He is a follower of Judaism and told me that the Jewish year that just began is considered a year of "shmita": traditionally, the one year out of every seven that a traditional farmer would let the land lay fallow, in order to allow the land to heal and recover - but metaphorically, a year when a period of rest is encouraged in a general way. Based on this idea, he wants to basically subsidize my salary for several months to allow me to leave my current position and take some time off for my family. Obviously, this is an idea not without some serious risk. I could see this as a fool's errand and a waste of time that would only damage my future salary or future career possibilities, but it could also be an opportunity to engage in serious study, with the potential of coming out the other side with marketable skills more befitting the economy of the future.

What are everyone's thoughts on this dilemma? What would you do if offered the same?

2. A cat's passing. Recently, one of our beloved family cats, Bryn, fell ill. The vets were worse than useless and I strongly believe that their medicine only weakened Bryn and hastened his decline. At the worst of it, I solemnly went out into the forest in our backyard and offered beer and fresh fruit to whatever spirit might live there. I asked the spirit that, if it were possible to bring Bryn back to health, if it could help Bryn on his path. I averred that I would continue to look for answers on the material realm, and I also told the spirit that if Bryn would come back to health, that I would take that as a sign that the spirit was real and had power. Well, Bryn didn't die - at least not at first. He made a full recovery and lasted 3 more months, then died of kidney failure, peacefully, while looking out our glass door. My question is... what happened here? Has anyone had a similar experience? I've chatted with the spirit (or my idea of the spirit) multiple times in the past 2-3 years - when we first moved I assured it that I would try not to cut down any trees or spend any more time in the forest than absolutely necessary, apart from clearing vines and man-made trash.

3. Druidry permission. I'd like to lead a discussion group where I chat about some of the precepts you've laid down in The Druidry Handbook. But I know that you have a working in place that prevents positive things from happening to people who don't properly pay for your work. I have a book that I've purchased through your manufacturer. Would I be allowed to send copies of certain excerpts of the book to my discussion group without violating your working? It would be about 10 people in the group total, I think.

4. A World Full of Gods. There's been a bit of crosstalk here about this one. Last I heard, you were considering revising it and doing a 2nd edition. Any updates on this, or should I go ahead and purchase the 1st edition? Has your worldview changed sufficiently since you wrote this book that you'd want to issue a disclaimer?

Thanks, as always, for the example you set and the work you do.

-Z

replies.title: Re: assorted questions

comments.title: Steiner's flaws?
comments.content:
I am just piggybacking on a comment of yours above...

What do you think are the biggest flaws of Steiner?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Steiner's flaws?
replies.content:
In general? I have no idea. I haven't read all his works and so don't propose to analyze the man based on what may be an incomplete sample of his views.

The problem I was referring to above is in his early, philosophical writings -- especially Goethe's Theory of Knowledge and The Philosophy of Freedom. Both are important books and worth studying, but Steiner was part of the attempt by post-Kantian philosophers to find some way to insist that the mind can know reality directly, rather than simply coming up with a model of reality that may or may not fit the ding an sich. Thus Steiner insists that the objects of thinking are completely transparent to consciousness, and that thinking about what we perceive through the senses gives us access to the actual meaning of those perceived things, and, er, he's wrong.

(1) The existence of subconscious mentation is extremely well documented, and subconscious mental activity plays a role in all our thinking and all the objects of our thinking; as Schopenhauer points out clearly in The Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, the fact that we continue to see optical illusions even after we understand that they're illusory shows that the objects of our awareness contain a level of mentation not accessible to our consciousness. (2) If Steiner's analysis was correct, nobody would ever make a mistake about the nature of a sensory phenomenon. Consider the movement of the Sun relative to the Earth. For many thousands of years, people watched that and put into it, via their thinking, the commonsense interpretation that the Earth is stationary and the Sun goes around it. They were quite wrong, of course. So was Steiner, on more than one occasion, when he tried to use thinking to understand the real nature of things he was perceiving. I've talked to more than one beekeeper who read Steiner's book on bees and said it was riddled with factual inaccuracies about the bees themselves.

That doesn't mean that Steiner's ideas are useless -- far from it. It means that his ideas need to be taken in context and revised with an eye toward the limits of human consciousness. Now that German Idealist philosophy can be put into context, so can Steiner, and his genuine insights extracted from his mistakes.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

In a reply here, and elsewhere, you've referred to Steiner's work as "brilliant but flawed." Would you be able to summarize the flaws in his esoteric work? Or at least a synopsis?

--David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I covered some of the issues in my response to Ahriman further up the thread. More broadly, Steiner -- like almost all the occultists of his time -- believed that the astral imagination properly focused would bring them genuine information about the prehistory of humanity and the planet, the nature of the spiritual world and its inhabitants, and so on. They hadn't yet grasped that the astral plane is as much a realm of dreams and illusions as anything else, and that it will feed your preconceptions back to you in a very convincing way! Thus most of what Steiner had to say about the evolution of the Earth and humanity is, well, no more accurate than any other account of those things from his time.

So his ideas need to be taken with a grain, and occasionally a whole tumbler, of salt. Fortunately a lot of the people who've developed his ideas worked hard to adapt them to the realities of the material plane, and so biodynamic farming, for example, and the Waldorf School system are both really quite good.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I was going to say this to the people asking about Steiner but you got there eventually. Basically like any flawed genius look to the ideas he started that have now had a long lineage in the outside world and have been applied by a large number of people with real results. There you will find his most valuable contributions.

comments.title: Alternate self
comments.content:
I don't know how best to write this without coming across as a crazy person, but is is possible to effectively be two people? I'm not talking about a split personality/multiple personalities etc, just that I feel like I have a 'night' mode and a 'day' mode. The day version is still me, but almost holds completely different viewpoints - so may feel a certain way about a topic, then night mode will be the opposite.

Night mode is more brooding, sociable, mischievous and generally more lazy, irritable and dissatisfied. He also really, really doesn't want to go to sleep, regardless of how tired I am. However, he is more creative, particularly in writing, and has an obsessive focus, such as reading for hours.

This obviously makes things like discussions with my wife quite difficult, as depending on when we have the discussion, the alternate version will be unhappy with whatever the outcome was. In addition, say I make plans to go to a party as the night version, the day version will then try to find any excuse not to go.

Note that I am fully conscious, it's not like one version blacks out or hands control to another, it's just that my thoughts gradually drift from one version to another pretty much around sunset or sunrise.

I am a Gemini, if that means anything. And pretty much everything about me fits the stock Gemini stereotype, as well as having a lot of Mercury and air-type associations. So maybe it's just my burden to bear for this incarnation that I can't properly decide on anything, or if I do, I will second-guess it endlessly.

Or perhaps I'm just quite sensitive to sunlight?

replies.title: Re: Alternate self
replies.content:
This sort of thing is almost universal, though most people hardly ever notice it in themselves--or if they do, they just write it off as different sides to one and their same personality or "self." Charles Godfrey Leland commented on his own experience of this sort of "multiple selves" in his "Gypsy Sorcery and Fortune Telling" (1891), chapters 11-12, in his "The Mystic Will" (1899), chapter 10, and at much greater length in his "The Alternate Sex, or the Female Intellect in Man and the Masculine in Woman" (1904). You can find PDFs on line of all these books. There are other, later authors who have commented on this, too.

A mage can use his own gaumet of alternate selves in magic (as Leland realized), since they all have different strengths and weaknesses: some of one's alternate selves are better suited to magic than others. The trick in to learn how to swap them out, one for another, seamlessly in one's own life.

This is by no means the same thing as "multiple / dissociatve personality disorder," which is an actual disorder that the person who has it finds hard or impossible to control, or at least hasn't figured out any means of controlling it -- let alone using it productively.

replies.title: Re: Alternate self
replies.content: You might try vitamin D supplements and see if they make a difference. I mean that quite seriously -- sunlight produces vitamin D in the skin, and that might be involved in the personality shift.
